summary:
Chichen Itza at its peak (c. 750–1200 AD) was the most powerful city-state of the Yucatan Peninsula and one of the great cities of the pre-Columbian Americas, developing during and after the [[event:2181|Classic Maya collapse]] as a center of post-Classic Maya civilization with distinctive architectural and cultural features suggesting strong connections to — or direct influence from — Central Mexican cultures, particularly the [[event:2182|Toltec Empire]]. The city's later construction phase, featuring the iconic El Castillo pyramid (Temple of Kukulcan), the Great Ball Court (the largest in Mesoamerica), and the Temple of the Warriors with its colonnades and Chac Mool sculptures, shows striking stylistic parallels with the Toltec capital of Tula.

El Castillo (the Pyramid of Kukulcan) is one of the most astronomically sophisticated structures in pre-Columbian architecture: on the equinoxes, the shadow pattern on the pyramid's balustrades creates the illusion of a serpent descending the staircase — a precise architectural calculation that required extraordinary mathematical and astronomical knowledge. The Sacred Cenote (natural sinkhole) at Chichen Itza was one of the major pilgrimage destinations in Mesoamerica, where precious objects of gold, jade, and copper — along with human sacrifices — were cast as offerings to the rain god Chaac; modern dredging has recovered thousands of artifacts from across Mesoamerica, demonstrating the city's role as a regional religious center. Chichen Itza was a major trading hub as well as religious center, controlling salt production from northern Yucatan coastal flats and trading networks extending throughout Mesoamerica. The city declined after about 1200 AD, when power shifted to Mayapan.
